text.title: Rate Limit Headers and 429 Responses
text.summary: IETF header names, 429 response shape, Retry-After semantics, and tiered limit examples.

rule.statement: Every rate-limited response (including 200s) must include RateLimit-Limit, RateLimit-Remaining, RateLimit-Reset, and Retry-After on 429 responses per the IETF draft.
rule.rationale: Well-behaved clients (Stripe SDK, axios-retry) auto-honor these headers and back off; without them clients hammer in tight retry loops.

example.code:
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests Retry-After: 60 RateLimit-Limit: 100 RateLimit-Remaining: 0 RateLimit-Reset: 1735689600 Content-Type: application/json
{"error": {"code": "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED", "message": "Too many requests", "retryAfter": 60, "limit": 100, "window": "1h"}}

section.title: Tiered Limits

rule.statement: Tier limit values are business decisions; agents must not finalize them — surface as a question to the human before implementing.
rule.rationale: Limit values affect pricing, conversion, and abuse surface. Wrong limits damage revenue or allow DoS.

section.p: Example tiered limit table (values are illustrative, not defaults):

rule.statement: Apply stricter limits to expensive endpoints (search, export, AI calls, OAuth mint) than to cheap read endpoints; document per-endpoint limits in the OpenAPI spec via x-rate-limit extensions.
rule.rationale: A single export call may consume 100x the resources of a status check; uniform limits either over-restrict cheap endpoints or under-protect expensive ones.

rule.statement: Whitelist monitoring and health-check IPs/keys; otherwise Pingdom and your own synthetic monitors will trip rate limits.
rule.rationale: Monitoring traffic is trusted and predictable; rate-limiting it causes false alerts and disguises real attacks.
